insight: Three false positives within 15 min of K514 Wing deploy shared two root causes: (1) overly broad invest* regex wildcard matching research/investigation vocabulary — word boundaries alone do not prevent semantic false positives; (2) DOTALL flag on .*? patterns allowing cross-line matching in large files, combining unrelated tokens across sections. Pattern: Augur pattern design must test against realistic production content, not only adversarial true-positive examples.

re.DOTALL + .*? is a false-positive factory when scanning large files. The combination allows matching token A near the top of a file with token B near the bottom, regardless of logical proximity. Fix: replace .*? with [^\n]{0,80} (same-line scope) for any pattern that requires two tokens to co-appear in meaningful proximity. DOTALL is appropriate for single-token patterns; never for multi-token co-occurrence patterns on large text surfaces.

Word boundaries (\b) prevent character-level false positives (investigate vs investing) but do not prevent semantic false positives (investing in research vs investing in our platform). A pattern like \binvesting\b matches both. Correct approach: enumerate the specific forbidden phrases rather than using a single broad alternation group. For invest*: the truly forbidden terms are investor/s (noun, specific), investment opportunity (specific pitch phrase), investing in our/the/your/this (verb in pitch context). Generic uses (investing time, investing in research, invest resources) are legitimate and should never trigger.

Wing Augurs should scan the diff (what changed), not the whole file. For StrReplace: new_string is the diff. For Write: the full content IS the new content (no old content available without a prior Read). The scan_scope: diff_only per-Augur field formalizes this. Implementation: ToolCall.diff_text holds new_string for StrReplace (or full content for Write). _text_to_check() routes to diff_text when augur requests diff_only scope. Security scanners (bandit, gitleaks) apply this same principle in code review — they scan the diff, not the entire file history.

Augur-Toolsmith was firing as an advisory on Bishop editing K-prompt files in BISHOP_DROPZONE/01_KnightPrompts/. Bishop edits K-prompts as normal scaffolding work — this is not Knight ratification. The pattern: advisory Augurs should fire only on the TARGET ACTOR for that advisory. Toolsmith advisory is for Knight (the ratifier), not Bishop (the scaffolder). Fix: scope Toolsmith advisory to REPORT_KNIGHT files and MILESTONE files only — the two contexts where Knight ratification actually occurs.

TimeWave Security (K517) accumulates rejected-action patterns in telemetry. In a test environment, the same 'FOR THE KEEP!' / 'Session closed' / 'K999 ratification complete' content runs repeatedly across many test sessions, building up rejection-pattern history. This causes TimeWave to escalate advisory→block on what should be purely advisory test scenarios. Pattern: Wing tests that verify advisory-only behavior should check 'augur_X in triggered_augurs' rather than 'decision == warn'. The consensus decision can legitimately be escalated by TimeWave in test environments; the augur signal itself is the invariant to test.

text_anti_patterns + require_anti_pattern_absent is a blunt instrument: if ANY anti-pattern is found anywhere in the content, the Augur entirely doesn't fire. This is appropriate for Augur-Toolsmith (if TS-### is anywhere in the ratification doc, the Toolsmith advisory is satisfied). It is LESS appropriate for Augur-Pricing (if 'API budget' appears anywhere in the file, pricing violations in other sections go unchecked). The Pricing fix uses line-scoped patterns ([^\n]{0,80}) as the primary fix; the anti-patterns serve only as a final backstop for common cost-context phrases that can't appear in real pricing-violation sentences.

K514.5 surfaces a gap in the Augur deploy protocol: new Augurs should be tested against production-realistic content BEFORE deploy, not only against adversarial examples. Recommended pattern: (a) true-positive suite (adversarial content that MUST trigger), (b) false-positive suite (realistic production content that MUST NOT trigger), (c) boundary suite (edge cases near the decision boundary). K514 only had (a). K514.5 adds (b) and (c) as the 8 Phase C checks. Future Augur additions should run all three suites before shipping.
